scope.summary: ALREADY IMPLEMENTED, verified rather than re-built. Both halves of this bead already ship in .github/workflows/release.yml: the plan §29.3 pre-tag release rehearsal (workflow_dispatch with every publishing job tag-gated) and the §27.2 C10 ratcheted binary-size budget (warn-then-fail with headroom instead of a hard cliff). This close records the verification; no code change was needed.
out_of_scope: Any modification of .github/workflows/release.yml: this close VERIFIES that file, it does not change it. It is listed in_scope because it is the artifact the claim is about.
Any change to .github/workflows/release.yml — none was required.
The driver-side half of Cluster B (§25.2 P0.1-P0.4 in rust-oracledb).
Actually dispatching a rehearsal run: triggering a workflow consumes CI resources and is an outward-facing action I did not have authorization to take. Verification here is static.

entry_points.name: release.yml workflow_dispatch — the pre-tag rehearsal path
entry_points.trace: `on:` resolves to ['push', 'workflow_dispatch'] under a YAML parse of the committed workflow (10 jobs). The header comment at release.yml:9-20 names this the PRE-TAG RELEASE REHEARSAL and cites plan §29.3 plus the three release-mechanics gates that cost the 0.9.0 tag its re-tag loop.

entry_points.name: publish-job tag gating — nothing ships from a rehearsal
entry_points.trace: release, publish-crates, docker and publish-mcp-registry each carry `startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/')`, verified by parsing each job body rather than reading the header comment. verify-mcp-registry carries no guard of its own but `needs: publish-mcp-registry`, so GitHub skips it transitively when its dependency is skipped. The gating jobs a rehearsal DOES run — checks, pinned-nightly, release-acceptance and the full 7-target build matrix including the binary-size check — carry no `if:` and therefore run on both triggers.

entry_points.name: ratcheted binary-size budget — warn band before the hard cap
entry_points.trace: release.yml:324-350 `Check binary size and musl static linkage` compares against two bounds: it exits 1 above matrix.binary_limit_bytes and emits `::warning::` above matrix.binary_warn_bytes. Six targets are 43000000 warn / 48000000 hard; the seventh is 36000000 / 40000000. Growth toward the cliff is visible run over run instead of surfacing as a tag-time failure.

limitations: Static verification only. I parsed the committed workflow and read the job bodies; I did NOT dispatch a rehearsal run, because triggering a workflow spends CI resources and is outward-facing. The claim proven here is that the rehearsal path exists and that no publishing job is reachable from it — not that a full rehearsal completes green today.

actionlint is not installed on this host, so the bead's 'actionlint clean' acceptance criterion was not executed. YAML well-formedness was confirmed with a PyYAML parse (10 jobs) instead, which is weaker: it catches malformed YAML but not GitHub-Actions-specific expression or context errors.

The `binary_warn_bytes` values are a point-in-time ratchet. Nothing here proves they are currently below actual binary sizes — if the binaries have already grown past the warn line, every release run is emitting a warning that nobody is acting on.
This close asserts prior work is correct; it adds no test. If the tag gating regresses, nothing in this repository fails — the guarantee rests on four `if:` expressions and one transitive skip.
follow_ups: Consider a cheap guard test asserting that every publishing job in release.yml carries the refs/tags guard, so the rehearsal's safety property cannot silently regress. Today it is convention, not an enforced contract.
Install actionlint in CI (plan §25.4 already proposes adding it to the cheap contracts job) so workflow edits get expression-level linting.
